Description: Two Heltec V3 nodes for personal validation before community commitment. No redundancy. Evaluation only.
Scalability Ceiling: 2 nodes — not scalable; no redundancy; evaluation only
Skill Barrier: Very Low — web flasher + smartphone app; no prior experience needed
Power Resilience: None — USB-C wall charger only; no battery/solar; fails if grid is down
Off-Grid Integration: Minimal — nodes can be powered from any 5V USB source including USB power banks
Disaster Applicability: Low — no redundancy; not suitable for actual crisis operations

Description: 5 T-Beam Supreme household nodes; 2 elevated with solar + IP65 enclosure. Covers 3–5 mile radius. Minimum viable community mesh.
Range per Node (Zone 5 flat terrain): 8–12 km per hop (upgraded antennas on elevated nodes); 2–5 km for mobile nodes
Scalability Ceiling: 25–30 node practical ceiling on LongFast preset before congestion tuning needed; adequate for this scenario
Skill Barrier: Low — one technically capable person per household can configure independently using smartphone app; 1–2 hours total for the cluster
Power Resilience: Good — 2 solar backbone nodes provide 24/7 relay indefinitely; household nodes run 24–48 hours on 18650 batteries
Off-Grid Integration: Direct: backbone nodes connect to home 12V bus via buck converter OR run on independent solar; household nodes charge from any 5V USB; compatible with off-grid-living 12V/48V systems
Disaster Applicability: High — tested architecture proven in Hurricane Helene recovery for exactly this use case; all nodes remain operational with grid down
Setup Time: 1 afternoon for initial setup; 1–2 hours for solar backbone node installation and testing

Description: 5 RAK WisBlock solar backbone nodes + 25 T-Beam Supreme household nodes + 5 mobile nodes. Covers 5–10 mile radius. Standard community resilience deployment.
Scalability Ceiling: 10–15 km backbone-to-backbone (6 dBi antennas at elevation); 2–5 km household node; full community coverage with 3–5 backbone nodes in Zone 5 flat terrain
Skill Barrier: 100 nodes on MediumSlow preset (switch from LongFast default); 35 nodes in this scenario is well within comfortable range; supports significant growth
Power Resilience: Moderate — requires one dedicated technical coordinator for backbone installation; household node setup remains smartphone-guided; solar node installation requires basic outdoor work skills and 2–4 hours per node
Off-Grid Integration: Excellent — RAK WisBlock backbone nodes run indefinitely on 5W solar + LiFePO4; household nodes provide 24–48 hour battery autonomy; full LiFePO4 chemistry for winter operation below -20C
Disaster Applicability: Full: backbone nodes integrate with off-grid-living MPPT controllers and battery banks OR operate on independent per-node solar; household nodes charge from house 12V bus; MQTT bridge to local Raspberry Pi for situational awareness dashboard
Setup Time: Very High — directly matches Am Mellensee deployment and post-Helene NC community model; proven architecture for real disaster response at this scale
column11: 2–4 weeks for full deployment including solar backbone installation and community training; 1 afternoon for initial test cluster

Description: 8–10 RAK WisBlock backbone nodes + 100 T-Beam Supreme household nodes + 10 mobile nodes. Covers 10–20 mile radius. Requires active channel management and community operating conventions.
Skill Barrier: 12–15 km backbone-to-backbone with 6–9 dBi directional antennas at highest elevations; full coverage of 10–20 mile radius with 8–10 backbone nodes in Zone 5
Power Resilience: ~150 nodes practical ceiling on MediumSlow before considering MeshCore for backbone; 120 nodes in this scenario is at the upper bound of manageable Meshtastic flood routing; requires disciplined Client Mute for all mobile nodes
Off-Grid Integration: Moderate-High — requires a dedicated network coordinator role (1 person managing firmware updates and node health for 110+ nodes); backbone installation requires coordination with property owners at elevation sites; operating conventions document required before crisis use
Disaster Applicability: Excellent — backbone infrastructure runs independently on solar/LiFePO4; 10-day battery autonomy on backbone nodes with no sun input; household nodes charged from community electrical infrastructure when grid down
Setup Time: Integrates with community microgrid infrastructure (see phase-6-community-scale-microgrid-design.md); backbone nodes can draw from community battery bank OR operate on independent solar; 100 household nodes represent negligible aggregate load (~15W total) on community power infrastructure
column11: High — matches Austin Mesh and Bay Area Mesh scale of deployment; proven in real city-scale operations; requires documented operating conventions (channel discipline; position interval policy; emergency message priority) to avoid the congestion failure mode observed in post-Helene NC during newcomer onboarding
column12: 1–3 months for full deployment; 2–3 weeks for backbone infrastructure; phased household onboarding over 4–8 weeks

Description: 15–20 backbone nodes (MeshCore firmware) + 150 T-Beam Supreme household nodes (Meshtastic firmware). Covers 20–40 mile radius. Protocol boundary between backbone and household tiers.
Skill Barrier: 15+ km backbone-to-backbone with directional antennas; 64-hop MeshCore backbone can span county-scale distances; household-tier coverage mirrors Scenario D per geographic cell
Power Resilience: Effectively unlimited for MeshCore backbone; 150 household Meshtastic nodes must be managed across geographic cells of ~40 nodes each with MediumSlow preset; this scenario exceeds what Meshtastic flood routing can handle as a single flat network — geographic segmentation is required
Off-Grid Integration: High — requires network architects who understand both Meshtastic and MeshCore protocols and can manage protocol boundary configuration; not appropriate for community organizations without dedicated technical volunteers; comparable in complexity to AREDN mesh deployment but without the ham license requirement
Disaster Applicability: Excellent — same solar/LiFePO4 architecture as Scenario D; at this scale distributed solar on backbone nodes is superior to relying on community grid infrastructure for backbone resilience
Setup Time: Full integration with community microgrid from phase-6-community-scale-microgrid-design.md; backbone nodes at geographic chokepoints receive power from nearest community solar installation; household nodes charge from distributed household solar systems
column11: High for crisis coordination — this is the scale of a county emergency management mesh; analogous to NC Mesh statewide deployment architecture; MeshCore backbone provides superior routing under the load that would collapse a flat Meshtastic network at this scale
column12: 3–6 months for full deployment; backbone design and site negotiation alone takes 4–8 weeks; household onboarding requires structured program with trained community tech volunteers
